comments.title: Things We Can Learn from Druidry and Three Cauldrons Question
comments.content:
Good Evening,

I hope everyone's week is going well, and if you are in the northern hemisphere, that it hasn't been /quite/ as hot as it is where I am - or at least that you've been able to find ways to deal with it, if so.

To Share: First off, thanks to everyone who shared thoughts last week on what has made Revival Druidry work over the years. I've taken the feedback I've gotten and built on the general thrust I gave about "what is Revival Druidry?" to put together a post on what we might learn about what has made it a successful, if fringe, religious movement: https://jpowellrussell.com/#what_might_we_learn_from_revival_druidry

To Ask: Within the last few weeks, I've started the Dolmen Arch version of the Three Cauldrons exercise, as given in the Gradd y Damcaniwr. Is it normal to feel one cauldron more strongly than the other two (in my case, the Cauldron of the Sun), and whether or not it's normal, is there anything to do about it besides keeping on with the exercise as given?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

To any who will have them, I put forth my blessings and best wishes,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Things We Can Learn from Druidry and Three Cauldrons Question
replies.content: It's entirely normal. You might make a little extra effort to feel the other cauldrons more clearly, but one way or another it'll balance out over time.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Greetings JMG
When planning, starting,, and finishing a project, at which point do you began to talk about it?
I ask because I have heard you say that you are not ready to talk about a project, and wonder why not?
Is there reasons to not invoke others when brainstorming?

A friend

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There are several reasons. First, in my experience the easiest way to kill a writing project is to talk about it before it's well under way. It's as though a certain amount of pressure needs to build up to make it happen, and talking about it lets the pressure trickle away.

Second, my ideas are always in conflict with the conventional wisdom, and when I discuss them, I can pretty reliably count on lots of people trying to push me back into something more conventional. Back when I was writing my deindustrial novel Star's Reach half a chapter or so at a time, I was constantly fielding comments from people who were trying to drag the story back into the future they wanted to imagine, which was of course one in which modern technology kept existing. My one and only feeler from a movie producer, which came in response to Star's Reach, did exactly the same thing: they wanted to take a story about what happens when it turns out that we're not going to the stars, and turn it into yet another dreary story about going to the stars. (I told the producer's flack to get lost.) The same kind of pushback happened while I was writing Retrotopia -- I was constantly fielding comments from people who insisted that I had to change the story to fit a more generic future. That gets very dull, and while a story's in its formative stages, it can mess things up.

The third reason is simply that I'm a very private person and don't like to talk much about my inner life. That's especially true of my creative work.

comments.title: French Tree(s) of Life
comments.content:
Hi,

A couple or three questions

1) Would you be able to recommend a text from the broader ‘French’/‘Continental’ tradition that goes into any detail about the attributions to paths on the Tree of Life - as distinct from those made popular through the English tradition/Golden Dawn et al?

I’m aware that Levi places the first decimal series of trumps on the sefiroth in Doctrine; and there are indications of an arrangement mapping trumps and connecting paths in Charlotte Cowell’s translation of G. O. Mebes’ Tarot Majors (the French-Russian source of Sadhu’s The Tarot); a very different diagram in Wilfried Houdouin’s Fundamentals; and yet another in a recent tarot deck that presents the ‘Lurianic’ attributions as the ‘French Tradition.’

I have not so far been able to find any actual texts dealing with the subject itself (I’ve tried Levi; Papus; Tomberg; Peladan; Wirth). It seems there might not be a consensus in the French context in general (Papus, Peladan and Wirth appear to diverge considerably), and a certain shared fondness, perhaps, for misdirection, but is there any actual discussion of the details? Or is it just a case of dealing with stray diagrams and strategic hints as an exercise for the reader to work through? (It is of course, very possible that I am missing something hidden in plain sight).

Could you or the commentariat suggest any useful pointers to any fuller presentation of the paths, whether in French or English?

2) Related: Are there any books/texts that go into exploratory/explanatory/meditative detail on the Lurianic tree - other than just indicating that it exists and what the basic mappings (elements, planets, constellations) are?

3) After the revival of interest in the Marseille tarot over the last ten years or so, there might be enthusiasm in the English-speaking world for more detail especially of a practical nature from the French/Continental context.

Given your course on Levi on the main blog, your Martinist lineage, and, I understand from some of your comments, your own work on Peladan, would you consider producing a book yourself on the French system(s) of an introductory nature similar to your own Learning Ritual Magic or Paths of Wisdom?

replies.title: Re: French Tree(s) of Life
replies.content:
1) The Golden Dawn approach -- all those multiply nested sets of symbolism mapped onto the Tree of Life -- was revolutionary in its time; it wasn't a common practice, and as far as I know, nobody has yet done the work of creating a comparable system using the French tarot correspondences. It could be done, but as far as I know, it hasn't yet been done.

2) How's your Hebrew? If such a book exists, it's not in English.

3) As noted under 1), the first step would have to be creating a coherent system along the lines of the GD system, but using one of the French symbolisms -- Wirth is probably the best starting place. It would be a whale of a lot of work. I'm not saying no, but I have a long list of other projects to get to already...

comments.title: Dreams and rebirth
comments.content:
I have been reflecting on waking from dreams and wondering if it's the same process as learning life again in a new body. I find, and perhaps everyone finds, that in the dream state, everything makes sense, or some degree of sense (depending on how much self reflection is coming from your mental body, which for me is an increasing amount these days), however upon immediate waking, nothing makes sense. The words that were in my dream, whilst in the same language, have no relation to real life words and concepts, and so on.

What got me thinking was that this is a shadow of the relearning process in a new body following rebirth. Words, actions, certain moral values etc all need to be relearned, no different to the emergence from a dreamstate, however the difference is of course the amount of time 'gone', and the lack of what I will call, lacking a better term, muscle memory.

Does this make sense to you?

replies.title: Re: Dreams and rebirth
replies.content:
Hey OP

Someone on here suggested Robert moss consciousness dreaming book to me. I found it very useful in deciphering dream symbols and unserstanding things spiritually. I don't usually have lucid dreams(I don't realize im dreaming)but I they are very vivid and totally functional. Upon waking I ponder on them throughout the day and write out as much as I can. Writing down what you can every morning helped me maintain dreams regularly. For me it helps things make sense.

replies.title: Re: Dreams and rebirth
replies.content: Hmm. I'd suggest a different comparison. Waking up is like dying; we leave the dream-life of the material world and awaken to our real life. Being born, in turn, is like falling asleep again, and going into a different dream. Just as we don't remember one dream when we slip into another, but can remember both of them on waking, we don't bring through knowledge from one life into another, but we can experience it all between lives.

comments.title: Natural magic and planetary invocation
comments.content:
Greeting JMG, you glorious host!!! :D

1. I was wondering about the possibility of creating magical items and I have some questions here how important is astrology here ? (in terms of exact astrological timing ?) If I make a talisman for love (for example) on the day and hour of Venus but Venus is then in aries is the talisman to be discarded?

2. I am professionally involved in herbalism, I am considering deepening my spiritual practice into natural magic, what books (besides your own, which is wonderful) could you recommend? And how much weaker is natural magic than, say, ceremonial magic? (I don't know if weaker is the right term, but based on, say, RPGs here, I'm wondering if natural magic is a waste of time and I should immediately explore ceremonial magic, of which, with the rest, natural magic is also a part). I have some experience in rituals and magic work.

3. I have noticed anger issues in myself, what planets could I invoke to deal with this? (and where can I read how to invoke these planets), will invocation based on SOP practice be suitable? I was thinking about writing poems towards the planets as a form of invocation, what do you think about it?

replies.title: Re: Natural magic and planetary invocation
replies.content:
1) You can start a fine quarrel among astrological magicians by asking this question! In my experience, the planetary hours are one source of power, and the actual planetary positions are another; you get the best results when you combine them, but if you do one and pay no attention to the other, you can still get results. For that matter, some people ignore timing altogether, and they still get results -- though it's chancier.

2) I recommend Cat Yronwode's books on hoodoo herb and root magic; they've been my go-to volumes since I studied with her. Natural magic is not as powerful as ceremonial magic, but it takes a lot less time to learn; if you begin practicing ceremonial magic now, it'll take a couple of years of daily practice and study before you can get serious results with it, whereas you can get results with natural magic right away. Thus I encourage people who are interested in practical magic to start with natural magic, and then assess their interest in the ceremonial path.

3) It depends very much on the sources of your anger. Anger is usually a secondary emotion -- that is, we get angry because it's less uncomfortable to feel anger than it is to feel the primary emotion underneath it, which is usually shame, guilt, or fear. You can't get past the anger until you deal with the primary emotion; invoking planets won't do the trick

This is why I recommend the practices of the Octagon Society, one of the traditions I studied with my late teacher John Gilbert; it's all about unpacking old emotions so that you can get past them and go on with your life. The Octagon work can be downloaded for free here:

https://octagonsociety.org/preliminary-lessons/

replies.title: Re: Natural magic and planetary invocation
replies.content: Many thanks for your answer, I Will look up to this Oxtagon society, beside natural magic what would be most,, practical magic"?

replies.title: Re: Natural magic and planetary invocation
replies.content: It really depends on how deep you want to get into magical practice and what kinds of magical work interest you. There isn't a nice neatly organized progression!

comments.title: Spiritual dimensions of the Burning Man community
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I was curious as to whether you or other commenters have experience or insights as to the metaphysical aspects of the Burning Man community. I've done some fulfilling creative work with burners and I'm wondering if it would be a good idea to deepen my association with them and/or attend the event.

The community seems to engage with people's needs that go completely unfulfilled by modern Western societies: a sense of organic community, ritual mysteries and a kind of "manufactured frontier" in an America where there's no actual frontier left for pioneers to explore. Their practice of building totems to parade at an annual ceremony is familiar to anyone who's studied world religions, though it's all framed as an art show which is less offensive to materialist sensibilities.

There is a strong sense of spirituality in the community and even some magical practice but it's all very haphazard without much in the way of a coherent method or clear goals other than throwing parties. People will organize rituals, with some believing they're just art and some believing they're more, but without banishing or making other efforts to prepare the etheric space.

Given that the entire culture and event are focused on the image of a giant burning human figure, could it be argued that the entire Burning Man egregore is directed toward self-destruction? I was definitely put off by the vixen-madness that overtook the community (and you had to be foxxed to attend!) but more and more of those rules are being memoryholed now. Those are some of my thoughts and observations, what do you think? I appreciate your time spent making these posts.

replies.title: Re: Spiritual dimensions of the Burning Man community
replies.content: I’m not JMG, but up until a few years ago I used to run into people in the Burning Man network all the time due to my work in a field adjacent to tech. I also knew several people from other areas of my life who entered that world. My experience is that everything affiliated with BM becomes hazy, unfocused, and energetically icky and dirty. I’ve always chalked it up to the heavy psychedelic drug use and intense focus on polyamory/sex. It’s not a clean scene on any level. Everyone I know who became heavily involved in it became someone I could no longer relate to. Fairly uniformly they all developed confused and messy personal lives, many became dependent on drugs and group drama. Not a good scene.

replies.title: Re: Spiritual dimensions of the Burning Man community
replies.content:
I've known many people who loved Burning Man and the community and have been involved for multiple years. Of the group, their lives fall into three main categories:

1. Very creative and successful (writer at a major magazine, scientist at Google and a major university, serial entrepreneur in furniture and design, etc.)

2. Very wealthy and successful (mostly financial sector people who fund enormous encampments at Burning Man)

3. Totally bonkers, not in a good way

I don't think the egregore is poisonous, just...know thyself. Will the creative energy and feelings of freedom gathered and released by Burning Man help and inspire, or drive you over the edge? YMMV.

It's also possible there are entities in the desert out there that use Burning Man to select individual victims (those people I know who've lost it.) Because it's in the American SW, where predatory entities are well known, this is worth keeping in mind and guarding against.

--Ms. Krieger

replies.title: Re: Spiritual dimensions of the Burning Man community
replies.content: Thanks, my initial impressions echo some of the things you and the above commenter have to say. The lack of cleansing practices certainly contributes to some of the ickiness and I've noticed the drama treadmill as well; in some burner circles there's a good deal of gossip and petty drama as background noise but it's not so pronounced in other groups. I've also heard rumors that there are a lot more freakouts and meltdowns at Burning Man itself than the outside world knows about, which could support the possibility of predatory entities in the desert. Perhaps the successful people are able to be "in it and not of it?" That may be the best way to approach the scene.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Good morning JMG; hope you are well today.

My questions: 1) what is the black sun in occultism?

2) how was/is it most commonly used in ritual?

3) why is it considered important?


Thanks in advance!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The Black Sun is one of the emblems of the old Ariosophist movement, an offshoot of Theosophy in late 19th century central Europe that embraced the idea that the Aryans were the Master Race. You've heard of that movement in another context; one of the largest Ariosophical organizations, the Thule-Gesellschaft or Thule Society, founded the Nazi party as its political wing in 1919 and recruited an Austrian vet with a funny little mustache to run it. These days, the Black Sun is used almost entirely by Neo-Nazi occultists -- and yes, the thought of white racists using a black sun as their symbol is just as funny as it sounds. If you want to know more about it and the movements that use it, Nicholas Goodrick-Clarke's book Black Sun: Aryan Cults, Esoteric Nazism and the Politics of Identity is the best place to start.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Once a week is a good frequency, and yes, you could do each of them for a month and -- once you've completed the sequence -- decide where to go from there.

2) There's an entire world of astrology between "traditional" (that is, Renaissance and older) and "modern" (that is, post-1950s psychological astrology). Writers to look for are Alan Leo, Llewellyn George, Robert De Luce, Max Heindel, and Ivy Goldstein-Jacobson, among others. This is the astrology that was practiced by the members of the original Golden Dawn, and I find it more accurate in its predictive power than traditional astrology (modern astrology rarely even makes a pretense of prediction). Horary is very useful for practical divination, and you should learn electional astrology in order to be able to choose good times for major rituals; natal is less important, though it's useful.

3) I recommend finishing your meditations up the tree. Then begin Pathworkings, and decide how soon to do them depending on how long it takes for you to meditate on what you encounter. Remember that meditating on your Pathworking experiences is as important, or maybe even more important, than doing the Pathworkings themselves!

replies.content: If you don't mind me asking, what mistake did you make?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's an ugly little story. In the third century AD I was incarnate as a young man in a city in what was then the eastern Roman empire; my family was moderately well-to-do, I had a good education, and as most people who weren't Christians did then, I was initiated into several of the old Mysteries. What I was supposed to do in that life was marry a certain girl from a much less wealthy family and then go to Athens and become active in the Pagan philosophy scene there; my wife would have come with me, of course, and she was a young woman of considerable intellectual talent who could have contributed much more than I ever would have. But my activities would also have accomplished certain things.

But my family wanted me to marry someone else, a girl from a rich and influential Christian family. I should have told them to get stuffed...but I didn't. I let them talk me into it, gave up my dreams of marrying the girl I wanted and going to Athens, and settled down instead to be a businessman and local politician in my home city. The kicker was that to marry the Christian girl I had to convert to her faith, and the Christians demanded of converts at that time that they betray the secrets of any Mysteries they'd been initiated into; that guaranteed that they wouldn't quit the church later, since the Pagans considered anyone who did that a person without honor, cursed by the gods.

So that's what I did. I betrayed the Mysteries for the cheapest and tawdriest of reasons, and -- without realizing it -- threw away several lives of preparation, and a lot of potentials for the future. The payback was very steep, and I made it worse by running from it for a good many lives, and doing various stupid things. So I had a lot of karma to work off just to get back to the point of being able to tackle the consequences of my mistake, and even after that it's been a matter of several lives of hard work. But here I am. ;-)

comments.content:
I had a TSW moment this past week, twice, that has shaken my worldview. One of my children is prone to nosebleeds every few weeks. They are prolonged and at times frightening. (But not serious; they come on when her nose is dry or after she’s had a cold that’s drying out.) I’m a follower of Christ and Mary but not a traditional one, nor baptized. I recently read a book about Braucherei (Penn. Dutch folk magic/powwow.) There were many traditional chants/prayers to stop bleeding.

My daughter had a sudden nosebleed, and I remembered pieces of one (involving three cool pools of water in the woods.) My daughter was panicking and I was frustrated I couldn’t remember the full prayer. I did remember the ending “Jesus, stop the blood. In the name of Christ, stop the blood.”

It stopped.

Like, right then, right there. A few more drops, that was it.

My daughter was stunned, I was stunned.

And then a few days later we went through the exact same scenario again. Again the blood stopped within seconds.

They are officially the shortest and second shortest nosebleeds she’s ever had.

I thanked Christ, lit candles, burnt frankincense. I journaled. I am grateful. But my worldview, which has always leaned toward the sacramental churches and away from the Protestants and Charismatics, is shook up. Not a bad thing. But I am trying to reorient myself.

Do you know of any trustworthy sources on this kind of experience? Especially healers’ memoirs? (Not that I consider myself a healer in that way, but I am interested to read others’ journeys through these experiences.)

Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The two best academic experts on Pennsylvania "Dutch" [ = German ] magic are the late Don Yoder (d. 2015, aged 93) and the much younger Patrick J. Donmoyer. They were each born into and raised in Pennsylvania German culture, and Donmoyer (at least) is also a fully trained traditional practitioner. (I'm not sure whether Yoder was one, too. He might well have been.)

Donmoyer's fullest account of the tradition is a substantial book, Powwowing in Pennsylvania: Braucherei and the Ritual of Everyday Life (2018). As a traditional practitioner, Donmoyer frankly admits that there are many things his culture's traditions of secrecy prevent him from disclosing in his publications, but what he can tell is substantial enough.

There is also David W. Kriebel's Powwowing among the Pennsylvania Dutch: A Traditional Medical Practice in the Modern World (2007), which I haven't gotten around yet to reading carefully.

Much less scholarly are books from various occult publishers such as Pendraig (C. R. Bilardi), Weiser ("Karl Herr," almost certainly a pseudonym) and Llewellyn ("Silver Ravenwolf," a known pseudonym).

Much of Pennsylvania German magic is a magic of specific charms. Most of these charms can be traced back for very many centuries, and were (still are?) found is all the Germanic languages (that is, all the Scandinavian languages, Dutch and Flemish, Low and High German, English). The use of charms for quite mundane purposes (healing, love, etc.), more generally, seems to be universal, or nearly so. Used skillfully, within a shared culture or a close relationship, they can indeed work -- even if one can't quite remember all the words in an emergency. Blood-stopping charms seem to be particularly effective. (I know of no academic studies that zero in on the physiological mechanisms involved in blood-stopping by charms, alas.)

TSW, most definitely.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
TSW!

I don't know any memoirs, but you might find it useful to read James McClenon's book Wondrous Healing, which argues that the ability of religious rituals to bring about sudden healing is one of the main reasons that religion exists in the first place.

comments.title: Implications of heliocentric astrology
comments.content:
The meditations on the Solar Logos in OPW resulted in the following ideas that I want to run by you to see if they make sense.

As a student of astrology, I understand each one the twelve Star Logoi as determining the character of one of the twelve astrological signs. For instance, when a planet is in Aries, its planetary spirit’s basic character is modified by the character of the Star Logos that governs Aries.

We are taught that the Sun is ruled by Leo and exalted in Aries. However, if we understand the Sun as the Solar Logos, the concepts of rulership, exaltation, and so forth don’t apply to it. As the Solar Logos the Sun works with all the Star Logoi. So what do we really mean by saying the Sun is exalted in Aries?

For that matter, what does it mean to say that the Sun is in Aries? Again, the Sun is above being in any of the signs.

While meditating on the illustration for this lesson I had some insights. Astrological practice developed out of a geocentric viewpoint. If we take a heliocentric viewpoint as in the illustration, from that standpoint when we say that the Sun is in Aries it means that the Earth is within the 30 degree sector radiating out of the Solar Logos that we call Aries. At that time the Earth is under the guidance of the Star Logos that governs Aries. From a heliocentric viewpoint we can say that the Earth is in Aries. Each time the Earth enters a different sign, while we say geocentrically that the Sun is in that sign, heliocentrically we mean that the Earth is in that sign, governed by that Star Logos.

Since each of the other planets has a basic character, like Mars as the planet of action and conflict, what is Earth’s basic character? If I lived on Mars, let’s say, and I was doing astrology there, I would be tracking the Earth, I would know what its basic character is, and I would understand how being in a sign would affect its basic character. Is there any way for us on Earth to know what Earth’s basic character is? Then I realized that there is a way. Let’s say that the Earth is in Aries. Although we say in astrology that the Sun is in Aries, it’s more like the Sun reflects back to Earth the fact that the Earth is in Aries. And since the Sun as the Solar Logos could be said to encompass all the signs and their characteristics, when we say that the Sun is ruled by Leo and exalted in Aries, that really means that the Earth is ruled by Leo and exalted in Aries. So there it is – Earth’s basic character. It makes sense, too: the Earth is flashy, showy, and attention-demanding, and spring is when it bursts with new life.

That answered another question: why does the spring equinox, 0 Aries 00, wipe the slate clean on mundane astrology? One of the meditations in this lesson tells us that each planet was created by one of the Star Logoi and still has a potent spiritual connection to the sign ruled by that Logos, the sign in which that planet is exalted. If the Earth is exalted in Aries as I’ve argued, then the Aries Star Logos acts on the Earth in a special way (the potent spiritual connection) when the Earth reaches 0 Aries 00, and one of the effects of that action is to reset the mundane astrological influences to begin anew at that time. It’s like an astrological spring cleaning.

Does this make sense? If it does, then it applies to astrology in other ways that I can work out for myself.

replies.title: Re: Implications of heliocentric astrology
replies.content: These are all intriguing ideas. I wonder, though.  If we see the Sun against the wedge of the sky associated with Aries, that means that at that moment the Solar Logos looking toward Earth would see her against the wedge of the sky associated with Libra. So the Earth just then is as far from Aries as she gets, and the Aries force is expressed through the Sun, which stands between the Logos of Aries and the Earth...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG

That maybe stupid question but... I'm from Poland with not a single drop of a celtic Blood in my veins, is it okay for me to practice druidry? Are Celtic Gods okay with slav?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's not a stupid question at all. The lines between Slavic, Germanic, Celtic, and Italic peoples became a political hot button after 1648, of course, but the further back you go before then, the harder it is to draw any line at all. The old Slavic thunder god Perun and the Celtic thunder god Taranis, for example, have versions of the same name -- it was Perkwunas back a millennium or so -- and Veles the earth god is pretty clearly the same as the Celtic Belinus.

In most Druid traditions these days, for that matter, it's not mandatory to use Celtic names for the gods and goddesses. If you want to use the names Perun, Veles, Devana, and so on, nobody's going to be upset by that, because Druidry isn't about this or that ethnicity -- it's about reverence for nature, and nature is everywhere.

However, these would still be blocked from returning to the US, where at least some of the energy would earth out in accomplishing the goals, and instead it's been building up in Canada, and it will continue to do so, until it finds a way to earth out.

Does this seem plausible to you?
